summary: This notebook demonstrates a mathematically derived optimal strategy for minimizing Brier Score in tournament prediction competitions. It shows how to load precomputed probability matrices generated from betting odds and apply a deterministic risk-taking rule to a base submission file. The author proves that assuming a team with a 33.3% win probability will win maximizes expected reward under the Brier Score metric. Readers should take away that strategic submission manipulation can outperform complex model training, and that precomputed odds-based probabilities serve as a highly effective baseline ingredient for medal-winning solutions.
competition_flow: Loads precomputed probability matrices and tournament seed data → applies a mathematically derived optimal risk strategy to a base submission CSV → outputs a modified submission file optimized for Brier Score.
data_reading: pd.read_csv loads probability tables, tournament seed CSVs, and the base submission file from Kaggle input directories.
data_processing: Drops the Elo_Rating column from probability matrices; parses team IDs and seed strings to map teams to tournament regions and rounds.
insights: Predicting a win for a team with exactly a 33.3% probability of winning maximizes expected reward under the Brier Score metric.
Precomputed probability matrices derived from betting odds can serve as a highly effective, model-free baseline for tournament predictions.
Tournament progression and round determination can be accurately reconstructed using only seed positions and region mappings.
